title: Daily Health Briefing
intro: Good morning! Here's today's research digest across your tracked topics — anti-aging, HIIT for women, pelvic floor training, emerging cancer therapies, and sleep. Each section summarizes recent, reputable findings, with sources linked as chips.

sections.body:
Two recurring "longevity" interventions got more nuanced human data — and both argue for caution over hype.

- **Rapamycin (PEARL trial):** The first long-term human RCT (the *Participatory Evaluation of Aging with Rapamycin for Longevity* study, ~115 adults aged 50–85, compounded rapamycin 5 mg or 10 mg weekly vs placebo for 48 weeks) found rapamycin was **well tolerated with no serious adverse effects**. The 10 mg dose was associated with improved **lean (muscle) mass in women** (~0.5 lb/month) and **bone mineral content in men**. Big caveats: it was effectively a **safety study, not powered for efficacy**, was funded by a company that sells rapamycin, and used compounded rapamycin that is ~3.5× *less* bioavailable than standard sirolimus — so the equivalent doses were closer to ~1.4 mg and ~2.9 mg.
- **Senolytics (D+Q ± fisetin):** A longitudinal pilot tracking **DNA-methylation "aging clocks"** found that 6 months of **dasatinib + quercetin actually *increased* epigenetic age acceleration** on first-generation/mitotic clocks (with shorter telomeres) — the opposite of the intended effect. Adding **fisetin** appeared to blunt that signal. Early, small, non-randomized — but a useful reminder that "senolytic" ≠ proven rejuvenation.

Bottom line: human longevity data remain **preliminary**. Favor interventions with real RCT support, and treat single-supplement anti-aging claims skeptically.

sources.title: Rapamycin slows muscle and bone aging in first long-term clinical trial (PEARL)
Safety and efficacy of rapamycin on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results
Effects of Dasatinib, Quercetin, and Fisetin on DNA methylation clocks: a longitudinal study

sections.body:
A new systematic review and meta-analysis (19 RCTs, **646 middle-aged and older women**, ages ~44–81, literature searched through Nov 2025) sharpens the picture of what HIIT does — and doesn't — do.

- **Cardiorespiratory fitness:** HIIT produced a **large, high-certainty improvement in VO₂max** (SMD = 1.20, 95% CI 0.86–1.54) across age groups, body-mass status, and program lengths.
- **Physical function:** HIIT did **not** significantly improve muscle strength, flexibility, or sit-to-stand performance; walking ability improved only borderline (low certainty). Conclusion: **HIIT alone is insufficient** to comprehensively improve functional performance.
- **Dosing in the studies:** **2–4 sessions/week**, programs from 2 weeks to 9 months, using cycling, treadmill, Nordic walking, or sport-based intervals.

Practical takeaway: keep HIIT for the strong cardio benefit, but **pair it with resistance training** to protect strength, balance, and function — especially through and after menopause. Ramp volume gradually and respect recovery.

sources.title: Effects of HIIT on cardiopulmonary fitness and physical function in middle-aged and elderly women: systematic review & meta-analysis
Effects of HIIT on physical fitness and body composition in recreationally active females

sections.body:
A large new randomized trial moves the conversation from *treating* incontinence to **preventing** it during pregnancy.

- **PEFLOW RCT (JAMA Network Open):** Across **9 hospitals in China**, **764 first-time mothers** were randomized to a structured **pelvic floor workout starting at 28 weeks' gestation** vs usual care. At 6 weeks postpartum, **stress urinary incontinence (SUI) was significantly lower** in the exercise group — **8.7% vs 13.9%** (risk difference ~5.2 percentage points, P = .03). More women also reached strong pelvic-floor strength (Modified Oxford Scale ≥4: **17.8% vs 7.9%**, P < .001).
- This reinforces that **pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT)** remains the evidence-based first line for SUI — and that starting *during pregnancy* can reduce postpartum leakage.

Key point: **technique and consistency beat raw reps.** Correct activation (ideally confirmed by a clinician or with biofeedback) is what drives results.

sources.title: Pelvic Floor Workout for Preventing Stress Urinary Incontinence in Primiparous Women: A Randomized Clinical Trial

sections.body:
**HER2-positive breast cancer — new FDA approval (June 24, 2026):** The FDA approved **palbociclib (Ibrance) + trastuzumab (± pertuzumab) + endocrine therapy** as **maintenance** treatment for HR-positive/HER2-positive locally advanced or metastatic disease after initial chemo. Based on the **PATINA trial** (518 patients), adding palbociclib **cut the risk of progression or death by 24%** (HR 0.76); it carries a breakthrough-therapy designation. Watch for neutropenia and (rarely) pneumonitis/ILD.

**TP53 / p53-mutant cancers:** p53 is still considered hard to drug, with **no broadly approved p53-targeted therapy** yet, but mutation-specific approaches are advancing:

- **Rezatapopt (PC14586)** — a first-in-class oral reactivator of the **TP53 Y220C** mutant — showed activity in the phase 2 **PYNNACLE** trial (SGO 2026). In heavily pretreated **ovarian cancer** (median 4 prior lines): **overall response rate ~44%**, median duration of response **8.2 months**, with **95% of patients showing reduced TP53 Y220C ctDNA** — supporting on-target effect.
- **Lonsurf + talazoparib** (an NCI-supported combo) selectively kills TP53-mutant cells by overwhelming their weakened DNA repair; in clinical testing.

Important framing: "alternative" should mean **evidence-based** options discussed with an oncologist — these targeted agents are not substitutes for standard care, and some remain investigational.

sources.title: FDA Approves Ibrance Combo for Maintenance of HR+/HER2+ Metastatic Breast Cancer (PATINA)
Rezatapopt Shows Activity in Pretreated Ovarian Cancer With TP53 Mutation (PYNNACLE, SGO 2026)
Drug Combination (Lonsurf + Talazoparib) May Have Potential for Cancers with TP53 Mutations

sections.body:
The evidence keeps favoring **behavioral therapy over supplements** for restless or disjointed sleep — and it's getting easier to access.

- **Digital CBT-I works at scale:** A large meta-analysis (**49 RCTs, 20,118 participants**) found that **fully automated, app/web-based CBT-I** significantly reduced insomnia severity vs controls (WMD −3.42 on the Insomnia Severity Index; 95% CI −4.35 to −2.48; P < 0.001). No therapist required — a scalable option when in-person CBT-I isn't available.
- **Melatonin — know its lane:** The AASM **advises against routine melatonin for *chronic* insomnia**; it's most useful for **circadian problems** (jet lag, shift work). OTC quality is also a concern: one analysis found actual melatonin content was off-label in **>71%** of products (ranging from 83% less to 478% more than stated).

Best-practice habits for disjointed sleep:

1. Keep a **fixed wake time**, even on weekends; allow time for **7+ hours**.
2. Use the bed **only for sleep**; if not asleep in ~20 min, get up.
3. Keep the room **cool, dark, quiet**; dim lights and cut screens ~30 min before bed.
4. If problems persist ≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months, see a provider to rule out conditions like sleep apnea and to start CBT-I.

sources.title: Effectiveness of Digital Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia: A Meta-Analysis of RCTs
Missing the mark with melatonin: finding the best treatment for insomnia
